content:
# Leviticus 20 — Disagreement Log

**Chapter:** Leviticus 20 (27 verses)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA
**Agents compared:** EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Total disagreements recorded:** 58

---

## Format

Each entry records: **Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | Ruling | Rationale**

---

## Disagreements

### D1 — V. 1 | Name form: Moses
- **EMET:** "Moshe"
- **LOGOS:** "Moses"
- **PNEUMA:** "Moses"
- **Ruling:** "Moses"
- **Rationale:** English name form per project convention. EMET's transliteration "Moshe" is consistent with its register but not the project standard for consensus text.

---

### D2 — V. 2 | Subject scope: "man" vs. "person"
- **EMET:** "Any man from the sons of Yisra'el and from the sojourner"
- **LOGOS:** "Any man of the sons of Israel or of the sojourners who sojourn in Israel"
- **PNEUMA:** "Any person — whether Israelite or foreigner living in Israel"
- **Ruling:** "Any man of the sons of Israel or of the sojourners who sojourn in Israel" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** MT *ish ish mi-bney yisrael* uses the doubled *ish* (man) in a distributive legal formula. PNEUMA's "any person" drops the *ish* and loses the legal register. LOGOS preserves both the formal subject and the complete scope of the law.

---

### D3 — V. 2 | "Seed" vs. "children"
- **EMET:** "gives from his seed [zar'o]"
- **LOGOS:** "gives any of his children"
- **PNEUMA:** "gives his children"
- **Ruling:** "gives any of his children" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** MT *mi-zar'o* (partitive: "from his seed/offspring"). EMET's "seed" is literal but unusual in an execution-of-children context. LOGOS captures the partitive *mi-* ("any of") which PNEUMA omits. "Children" is the natural English referent for *zera'* when actual progeny are meant.

---

### D4 — V. 2 | Transliterations throughout: Yisra'el, Molekh
- **EMET:** "Yisra'el," "Molekh" (transliterated Hebrew throughout)
- **LOGOS:** "Israel," "Molech"
- **PNEUMA:** "Israel," "Molech"
- **Ruling:** "Israel," "Molech" (LOGOS/PNEUMA)
- **Rationale:** English name forms per project convention. EMET's transliterations are appropriate for its register but not the consensus standard.

---

### D5 — V. 2 | "Stone him with stones" vs. "stone him"
- **EMET:** "shall stone him with stones [ragom yirgemu oto ba-aven]"
- **LOGOS:** "shall stone him with stones"
- **PNEUMA:** "shall stone him"
- **Ruling:** "shall stone him with stones" (EMET/LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** MT *ba-aven* ("with stones") is present and should be preserved. PNEUMA drops "with stones," simplifying beyond the text. The specific means of execution is part of the legal specification.

---

### D6 — V. 3 | Emphatic "I myself" vs. "And I — I"
- **EMET:** "And I — I will set my face [ani eten et-panai]"
- **LOGOS:** "I myself will set my face against"
- **PNEUMA:** "I myself will set my face against"
- **Ruling:** "I myself will set my face against" (LOGOS/PNEUMA)
- **Rationale:** The emphatic *ani* pronoun standing separately before the verb is captured by "I myself." EMET's dash construction ("And I — I") is accurate but less idiomatic in running English. The consensus uses "I myself" for this emphatic *ani* construction, following the pattern established in LOGOS.

---

### D7 — V. 3 | "From his seed" vs. "one of his children"
- **EMET:** "for from his seed he gave to the Molech"
- **LOGOS:** "because he has given one of his children to Molech"
- **PNEUMA:** "because he gave his children to Molech"
- **Ruling:** "because he has given one of his children to Molech" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** MT repeats *mi-zar'o* (partitive "from his seed") from v. 2. LOGOS captures the partitive. PNEUMA drops it. EMET's "seed" is literal but contextually awkward. The perfect tense "has given" (LOGOS) is appropriate for the grounds-clause.

---

### D8 — V. 3 | "Defiling" vs. "to defile" / "so as to defile"
- **EMET:** "so as to defile my sanctuary"
- **LOGOS:** "defiling my sanctuary"
- **PNEUMA:** "defiling my sanctuary"
- **Ruling:** "defiling my sanctuary" (LOGOS/PNEUMA)
- **Rationale:** MT *le-tame et-miqdashi* = purpose/result infinitive construct. Both "defiling" (participial result) and "so as to defile" (purposive) are defensible. "Defiling" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is more idiomatic in English and flows more naturally in the sentence.

---

### D9 — V. 4 | "Close their eyes" vs. "hide their eyes"
- **EMET:** "hide their eyes [ha'lem ya'limu]"
- **LOGOS:** "close their eyes"
- **PNEUMA:** "close their eyes" / "look the other way"
- **Ruling:** "close their eyes" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** *Ha'lem ya'limu* = infinitive absolute of *alam* ("to conceal/hide"). "Hide their eyes" (EMET) is more literal; "close their eyes" is the natural English idiom for deliberate willful blindness. "Look the other way" (PNEUMA) is paraphrase. The consensus uses the idiomatic "close their eyes."

---

### D10 — V. 4 | "At all close" — infinitive absolute emphasis
- **EMET:** "do at all hide their eyes" (preserving inf. absolute)
- **LOGOS:** "at all close their eyes"
- **PNEUMA:** "close their eyes" (drops emphasis)
- **Ruling:** "close their eyes" — the "at all" marker is dropped in the consensus
- **Rationale:** The infinitive absolute emphasis (*ha'lem ya'limu* = "hiding they shall hide") is linguistically significant but the English "at all" is slightly awkward. The consensus chooses idiomatic English and records the emphasis here.

---

### D11 — V. 5 | "Family" vs. "clan"
- **EMET:** "his family [mishpachto]"
- **LOGOS:** "his clan"
- **PNEUMA:** "his family"
- **Ruling:** "his family" (EMET/PNEUMA)
- **Rationale:** *Mishpachah* = extended family/kin-group. "Family" is the project's established rendering for *mishpachah* throughout. "Clan" (LOGOS) is used in some translations but introduces an anthropological precision not needed here.

---

### D12 — V. 5 | "Whoring after him" vs. "follow him in whoring" vs. "prostitute themselves"
- **EMET:** "all who go whoring after him [ha-zonim acharav], whoring after the Molech"
- **LOGOS:** "all who follow him in whoring after Molech" (collapses double whoring)
- **PNEUMA:** "all who prostitute themselves after him by chasing after Molech"
- **Ruling:** "all who go whoring after him, whoring after Molech" (EMET, preserving MT repetition)
- **Rationale:** The MT has a deliberate double use of *zanah* — "whoring after him, whoring after Molech." This rhetorical repetition should be preserved. LOGOS collapses it to one instance. PNEUMA's "prostitute themselves...chasing" is a paraphrase. "Go whoring after" is the project's established rendering for the *zanah acharei* idiom.

---

### D13 — V. 5 | "From among their people" — cut-off scope
- **EMET:** "from among their people" (end of clause)
- **LOGOS:** "from among his people, him and all who follow him in whoring after Molech" (restructured)
- **PNEUMA:** "I will cut them off from their people" (second cut-off verb added)
- **Ruling:** "I will cut him off from among his people — him and all who go whoring after him, whoring after Molech" (adjusted LOGOS structure)
- **Rationale:** The MT has one *ve-hikhratiti* verb with an extended object: "him and all who go whoring after him." The pronoun scope ends with "from among their people." LOGOS's restructuring is the clearest in English. PNEUMA adds a second "I will cut them off" not in the MT.

---

### D14 — V. 6 | "Person" — article and specificity
- **EMET:** "the person [ha-nefesh]"
- **LOGOS:** "a person"
- **PNEUMA:** "anyone"
- **Ruling:** "a person" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** *Ha-nefesh* is a legal subject term for the individual. The definite article in Hebrew is a generic/categorical definite ("the person who..."), rendered naturally as "a person" in English. EMET's "the person" is technically accurate but requires an antecedent. PNEUMA's "anyone" is colloquial and loses the *nefesh* register.

---

### D15 — V. 6 | "Spirits of the dead" vs. "necromancers" vs. "spiritists"
- **EMET:** "the spirits of the dead [ha-yid'onim]"
- **LOGOS:** "necromancers"
- **PNEUMA:** "spiritists"
- **Ruling:** "spirits of the dead" (EMET)
- **Rationale:** *Ha-yid'onim* (from *yada'*, "to know") denotes "knowers" — spirits or practitioners associated with the dead. EMET's "spirits of the dead" is the most literal. "Necromancers" (LOGOS) is interpretive. "Spiritists" (PNEUMA) is too vague. The consensus renders *yid'onim* as "spirits of the dead" throughout (vv. 6 and 27) to preserve the distinction from *ovot* ("mediums").

---

### D16 — V. 7 | "Sanctify yourselves" vs. "Consecrate yourselves"
- **EMET:** "you shall sanctify yourselves [ve-hitqadishtem]"
- **LOGOS:** "Consecrate yourselves"
- **PNEUMA:** "Consecrate yourselves"
- **Ruling:** "you shall sanctify yourselves" (EMET)
- **Rationale:** The Hithpael *hitqadishtem* is from the root *q-d-sh* ("to be/make holy"), the same root as *qedoshim* ("holy") in the same verse and *meqadishkhem* ("who sanctifies you") in v. 8. The triple root recurrence in vv. 7–8 is a deliberate semantic chain. "Sanctify yourselves / holy / sanctifies you" preserves this unity. "Consecrate" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) breaks the verbal chain, since they both use "sanctifies" in v. 8.

---

### D17 — V. 7 | "And be holy" vs. "be holy"
- **EMET:** "and you shall be holy"
- **LOGOS:** "be holy"
- **PNEUMA:** "be holy"
- **Ruling:** "and be holy" (EMET)
- **Rationale:** MT has *vi-heyitem qedoshim* — the *vav* conjunction is present. "And be holy" preserves the coordinate structure of the Hebrew command pair. LOGOS and PNEUMA drop the conjunction, reading as asyndeton.

---

### D18 — V. 8 | "Carry them out" vs. "do them"
- **EMET:** "you shall do them"
- **LOGOS:** "do them"
- **PNEUMA:** "carry them out"
- **Ruling:** "do them" (EMET/LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** *Va-asitem otam* = plain *asah* ("to do/make"). "Do them" is the direct equivalent. "Carry them out" (PNEUMA) is a compound that imports an additional sense of execution/implementation not present in the simple *asah*.

---

### D19 — V. 9 | "Anyone who curses" vs. "any man who curses"
- **EMET:** "any man who curses [ish ish asher yeqallel]"
- **LOGOS:** "anyone who curses"
- **PNEUMA:** "anyone who curses"
- **Ruling:** "anyone who curses" (LOGOS/PNEUMA)
- **Rationale:** MT *ish ish* = distributive "any man / every man." The distributive *ish ish* has generic force in legal contexts. "Anyone" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) captures this without implying gender restriction. "Any man" (EMET) could be read as excluding women from this law's scope.

---

### D20 — V. 9 | "Blood is upon him" vs. "blood is on his own head"
- **EMET:** "his blood is upon himself [damav bo]"
- **LOGOS:** "his blood is upon him"
- **PNEUMA:** "his blood is on his own head"
- **Ruling:** "his blood is upon him" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** MT *damav bo* = "his blood [is] upon him." LOGOS is the direct equivalent. EMET's "upon himself" is reflexively redundant. PNEUMA's "on his own head" adds "head" with no basis in the Hebrew *bo*. This formula recurs in vv. 11, 12, 13, 16, 27 and the consensus applies "upon him/them" consistently.

---

### D21 — V. 10 | Clause structure — double "with the wife of"
- **EMET:** "a man who commits adultery with the wife of another man — who commits adultery with the wife of his neighbor"
- **LOGOS:** "If a man commits adultery with the wife of his neighbor"
- **PNEUMA:** "If a man commits adultery with another man's wife — with the wife of his neighbor"
- **Ruling:** "If a man commits adultery with the wife of his neighbor" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** The MT appears to have a repetitive clause (*ish asher yina'ef et-eshet ish... et-eshet re'ehu*). EMET preserves the repetition verbatim; LOGOS streamlines to "his neighbor" from the second clause; PNEUMA partially preserves both. The consensus streamlines with LOGOS, recording the MT's repetitive structure here.

---

### D22 — V. 10 | "Adultery with the wife of his neighbor" — opening conditional
- **EMET:** "And a man who commits adultery... the adulterer and the adulteress shall surely be put to death"
- **LOGOS:** "If a man commits adultery..."
- **PNEUMA:** "If a man commits adultery..."
- **Ruling:** "If a man..." (LOGOS/PNEUMA)
- **Rationale:** The penal-code laws of vv. 10–21 use conditional *im/ki* constructions in the MT. LOGOS and PNEUMA consistently render these with "If a man..." which is the correct conditional form. EMET uses relative-clause constructions ("a man who...") which are more literal to the MT's participial style but less natural in English legal idiom for a series of conditions.

---

### D23 — V. 11 | "Lies with his father's wife" — nakedness formula
- **EMET:** "the nakedness of his father he has uncovered"
- **LOGOS:** "he has uncovered his father's nakedness"
- **PNEUMA:** omits; renders as "he has violated his own father's honor"
- **Ruling:** "he has uncovered his father's nakedness" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** The *gilui ervah* formula ("uncovering the nakedness") is the project-mandated legal terminology that must be preserved throughout the penal code. PNEUMA's substitution of "violated his own father's honor" eliminates the technical term and is inadmissible.

---

### D24 — V. 11 | "Sleeps with" vs. "lies with"
- **EMET:** "a man who lies with the wife of his father"
- **LOGOS:** "If a man lies with his father's wife"
- **PNEUMA:** "If a man sleeps with his father's wife"
- **Ruling:** "lies with" (EMET/LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** *Shakav* is the standard legal technical verb for intercourse throughout the penal code of Leviticus 20 (vv. 11, 12, 13, 15, 16, 18, 20). "Lies with" is the established formal equivalent. PNEUMA's "sleeps with" is more colloquial and loses the uniform legal register of *shakav*.

---

### D25 — V. 12 | "Perversion" vs. "confusion"
- **EMET:** "a confusion [tevel]"
- **LOGOS:** "a perversion"
- **PNEUMA:** "a perversion"
- **Ruling:** "a perversion" (LOGOS/PNEUMA)
- **Rationale:** *Tevel* (hapax/near-hapax, possibly from *balal* "to mix/confuse") denotes violation of categorical order. "Perversion" captures both the structural violation and the moral valence. "Confusion" (EMET) is more etymological but less semantically adequate in English. ESV, NASB use "perversion."

---

### D26 — V. 13 | "Male" vs. "man" for zachar
- **EMET:** "with a male [zachar]"
- **LOGOS:** "with a male"
- **PNEUMA:** "with a man"
- **Ruling:** "with a male" (EMET/LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** MT *zachar* is the sex-term ("male," as in Gen 1:27 *zachar u-neqevah*), not *ish* (a man). The legal precision of *zachar* as a sex-classification term must be preserved. PNEUMA's "man" loses this precision.

---

### D27 — V. 13 | "Something abominable" vs. "an abomination"
- **EMET:** "an abomination [to'evah] they have done"
- **LOGOS:** "both of them have committed an abomination"
- **PNEUMA:** "both of them have done something abominable"
- **Ruling:** "both of them have committed an abomination" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** *To'evah* is a noun ("abomination"), not an adjective ("abominable"). PNEUMA's "something abominable" weakens the categorical noun to an adjectival predicate, losing the full weight of the term. LOGOS preserves the noun. Word order follows LOGOS for natural English.

---

### D28 — V. 14 | "Her mother also" vs. "both a woman and her mother"
- **EMET:** "a woman and her mother [imah]"
- **LOGOS:** "a woman and her mother also"
- **PNEUMA:** "both a woman and her mother"
- **Ruling:** "a woman and her mother also" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** LOGOS's "also" reflects the MT's conjunctive structure (the mother is added to the primary relationship). PNEUMA's "both" is an addition beyond the Hebrew that re-frames the sequence.

---

### D29 — V. 14 | "All three" vs. "he and they"
- **EMET:** "in fire they shall burn him and them"
- **LOGOS:** "he and they shall be burned with fire"
- **PNEUMA:** "All three must be burned with fire"
- **Ruling:** "he and they shall be burned with fire" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** MT *oto ve-ethen yisrefu* ("him and them they shall burn"). "He and they" (LOGOS) follows the MT exactly. "All three" (PNEUMA) is interpretively accurate but introduces a numerical specification not in the Hebrew. The MT's "him and them" deliberately maintains the distinction in reference.

---

### D30 — V. 14 | Active vs. passive construction for burning penalty
- **EMET:** "in fire they shall burn him and them" (active)
- **LOGOS:** "shall be burned with fire" (passive)
- **PNEUMA:** "must be burned with fire" (passive)
- **Ruling:** "shall be burned with fire" (LOGOS/passive)
- **Rationale:** MT *yisrefu* is active 3mp ("they shall burn"), but the passive is conventional in English legal idiom for execution sentences, following the pattern of "shall be put to death" throughout. LOGOS and PNEUMA agree on passive. EMET's active preserves the MT form.

---

### D31 — V. 15 | "Lies with an animal" vs. "gives his lying to an animal" vs. "has sexual relations"
- **EMET:** "a man who gives his lying [shekhovto] to an animal"
- **LOGOS:** "If a man lies with an animal"
- **PNEUMA:** "If a man has sexual relations with an animal"
- **Ruling:** "If a man lies with an animal" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** MT *asher yiten shekhovto ba-behemah* = "who gives his lying [sexual intercourse] with an animal." EMET's "gives his lying" is literal but awkward. LOGOS's "lies with" is the standard equivalent of *shakav* and consistent with the chapter's legal vocabulary. PNEUMA's "has sexual relations with" is accurate but more circumlocutory than the Hebrew warrants.

---

### D32 — V. 16 | "So that it lies with her" vs. "and lies with it" — grammatical agency
- **EMET:** "so that it lies [le-riv'ah] with her"
- **LOGOS:** "and lies with it" (woman as grammatical agent of lying)
- **PNEUMA:** "to mate with it" (neutral)
- **Ruling:** "so that it lies with her" (EMET)
- **Rationale:** MT *le-riv'ah ota* — the infinitive construction specifies the animal as agent of the sexual act (the animal mounts her). LOGOS's "and lies with it" grammatically makes the woman the subject of *lies*, reversing the Hebrew agency. PNEUMA's "to mate with it" is gender-neutral but accurate enough. EMET's "so that it lies with her" correctly preserves the MT's assignment of agency.

---

### D33 — V. 17 | "Sees her nakedness / she sees his" — PNEUMA omits
- **EMET:** "and sees her nakedness and she sees his nakedness"
- **LOGOS:** "and sees her nakedness, and she sees his nakedness"
- **PNEUMA:** omits; "they have sexual relations"
- **Ruling:** "and sees her nakedness, and she sees his nakedness" (EMET/LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** The *ra'ah ervah* formula (seeing nakedness rather than uncovering it) is the specific legal vocabulary of v. 17, used in place of the standard *galah ervah*. The reciprocal double clause ("he sees / she sees") is present in the MT and must be preserved. PNEUMA's substitution of "have sexual relations" eliminates both the technical formula and the MT's emphasis on the mutual act of exposure.

---

### D34 — V. 17 | "Disgrace" vs. "shameful thing"
- **EMET:** "it is a disgrace [chesed hu]"
- **LOGOS:** "it is a shameful thing"
- **PNEUMA:** "that is a shameful thing"
- **Ruling:** "it is a disgrace" (EMET)
- **Rationale:** *Chesed* in v. 17 is the single most lexically anomalous term in the chapter — the only place in the Hebrew Bible where *chesed* clearly has a negative meaning ("disgrace/shame"). "Disgrace" (EMET) renders the Hebrew as a noun paralleling the noun *chesed*. "Shameful thing" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) adjectivizes the noun. "Disgrace" is the more established rendering (KJV "wickedness"; RSV/NRSV/ESV "disgrace") and mirrors the compact Hebrew noun-predicate *chesed hu*. The LXX *atapon estin* ("it is improper") confirms the pejorative reading.

---

### D35 — V. 17 | "Cut off in the sight of" vs. "publicly cut off"
- **EMET:** "they shall be cut off [ve-nikhreitu] in the eyes of the sons of their people"
- **LOGOS:** "they shall be cut off in the sight of the children of their people"
- **PNEUMA:** "They must be publicly cut off from their people"
- **Ruling:** "they shall be cut off in the sight of the children of their people" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** V. 17 has an unusual *karet* formula: *ve-nikhreitu le-einei bney ammam* ("they shall be cut off before the eyes of the children of their people"). This variant form — with *le-einei* ("before the eyes of") rather than the standard *mi-qerev ammam* ("from among their people") — is significant and must be preserved verbatim. PNEUMA's "publicly cut off from their people" paraphrases, adds "publicly" (not in Hebrew), and omits the distinctive *bney ammam* ("children of their people"). LOGOS preserves the formula exactly.

---

### D36 — V. 17 | "He has uncovered the nakedness of his sister" — PNEUMA deviates
- **EMET:** "the nakedness of his sister he has uncovered — his iniquity he shall bear"
- **LOGOS:** "He has uncovered his sister's nakedness; he shall bear his iniquity"
- **PNEUMA:** "He has violated his own sister — he will bear the guilt of it"
- **Ruling:** "He has uncovered the nakedness of his sister — he shall bear his iniquity" (LOGOS/EMET adjusted)
- **Rationale:** The *gilui ervah* formula must be preserved. PNEUMA's "violated" eliminates the technical legal language. "Iniquity" (*avon*) is preferred over PNEUMA's "guilt" — *avon* is a specific term for moral debt/iniquity, weightier than generic "guilt."

---

### D37 — V. 18 | "Menstrual impurity" vs. "sickness of her impurity" vs. "monthly period"
- **EMET:** "the sickness of her impurity [dovah]"
- **LOGOS:** "menstrual impurity"
- **PNEUMA:** "her monthly period"
- **Ruling:** "menstrual impurity" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** MT *be-dovata* ("in her sickness/infirmity") specifically describes the menstrual condition. "Menstrual impurity" (LOGOS) is the accurate idiomatic English equivalent, naming both the type and the state. "Sickness of her impurity" (EMET) is literal but awkward. "Monthly period" (PNEUMA) is too casual for the legal register and drops the impurity (*tum'ah*) dimension.

---

### D38 — V. 18 | "He has laid bare her flow" — structure
- **EMET:** "her flow he has laid bare, and she has uncovered the flow of her blood"
- **LOGOS:** "he has laid bare her flow, and she has uncovered the flow of her blood"
- **PNEUMA:** "he has exposed the source of her flow and she has uncovered it"
- **Ruling:** "he has laid bare her flow, and she has uncovered the flow of her blood" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** PNEUMA's "source of her flow" and "uncovered it" lose the parallel structure and eliminate the second mention of "flow of her blood" (*meqor dameha*) which the MT explicitly states. LOGOS preserves both parallel clauses.

---

### D39 — V. 19 | "And" vs. "or" between mother's sister / father's sister
- **EMET:** "the nakedness of your mother's sister and of your father's sister" ("and")
- **LOGOS:** "the nakedness of your mother's sister or of your father's sister" ("or")
- **PNEUMA:** "your mother's sister or your father's sister" ("or")
- **Ruling:** "or" (LOGOS/PNEUMA)
- **Rationale:** MT has *o* (or) between the two relations: *ve-ervat achat imkha ve-achat avikha* — EMET may be reading the *ve-* as "and," but the standard reading of the *o* (or) in the prohibition formula is disjunctive. LOGOS and PNEUMA correctly render "or."

---

### D40 — V. 19 | "Lay bare one's own flesh" vs. "violate a close blood relative"
- **EMET:** "for his own flesh [she'ero] he has laid bare"
- **LOGOS:** "for that is to lay bare one's own flesh"
- **PNEUMA:** "for that is to violate a close blood relative"
- **Ruling:** "for that is to lay bare one's own flesh" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** MT *ki she'era gilla* — *she'er* = "flesh/near kin"; *gilla* = "to uncover/lay bare." "Lay bare one's own flesh" (LOGOS) captures both the literal bodily sense and the kinship dimension. PNEUMA's "violate a close blood relative" is a valid interpretive paraphrase but eliminates the *galah* (uncover/lay bare) verb and the *she'er* (flesh) noun, both of which are legally significant.

---

### D41 — V. 19 | PNEUMA omits "nakedness" from the prohibition
- **EMET:** "the nakedness of your mother's sister... you shall not uncover"
- **LOGOS:** "the nakedness of your mother's sister or of your father's sister"
- **PNEUMA:** "Do not have sexual relations with your mother's sister or your father's sister"
- **Ruling:** preserve "nakedness" formula (EMET/LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** The *gilui ervah* formula must be preserved. PNEUMA's "Do not have sexual relations" eliminates the technical legal language. The prohibition specifically names "nakedness" (*ervah*) as the project-mandated terminology.

---

### D42 — V. 20 | "Bear their sin" vs. "bear the guilt"
- **EMET:** "they shall bear their sin [chet'am yis'u]"
- **LOGOS:** "they shall bear their sin"
- **PNEUMA:** "They will bear the guilt"
- **Ruling:** "they shall bear their sin" (EMET/LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** *Chet'* is specifically "sin" (the nominal form), a more precise term than generic "guilt." EMET and LOGOS agree. PNEUMA's "guilt" is too general.

---

### D43 — V. 20 | "Shall die childless" — word order
- **EMET:** "childless [aririn] they shall die"
- **LOGOS:** "shall die childless"
- **PNEUMA:** "they will die childless"
- **Ruling:** "shall die childless" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** "Shall die childless" is the most natural English word order for the penalty formula. EMET's "childless they shall die" follows Hebrew word order. PNEUMA's "will die childless" uses a weaker modal. "Shall" (LOGOS) is appropriate for legal prescription.

---

### D44 — V. 21 | "Marries" vs. "takes"
- **EMET:** "a man who takes the wife of his brother [eshet achiv]"
- **LOGOS:** "If a man takes his brother's wife"
- **PNEUMA:** "If a man marries his brother's wife"
- **Ruling:** "If a man takes his brother's wife" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** MT *ve-ish asher yiqach et-eshet achiv* — the verb *laqach* ("to take") is the standard biblical legal term for acquiring a wife; it does not specify whether the relationship is formal marriage or simply intercourse. PNEUMA's "marries" is an interpretive specification beyond the text.

---

### D45 — V. 21 | "He has uncovered his brother's nakedness" — PNEUMA deviates
- **EMET:** "the nakedness of his brother he has uncovered — childless they shall be"
- **LOGOS:** "he has uncovered his brother's nakedness — they shall be childless"
- **PNEUMA:** "He has violated his brother — they will be childless"
- **Ruling:** "he has uncovered his brother's nakedness — they shall be childless" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** The *gilui ervah* formula must be preserved. PNEUMA's "violated" eliminates the technical term. Additionally, the penalty in v. 21 is *arurim yihyu* ("they shall be childless") — without the *yamut* ("shall die") of v. 20. LOGOS correctly renders "they shall be childless" (no "die"). EMET also correctly omits "die" here.

---

### D46 — V. 22 | "Statutes and rules" vs. "statutes and judgments" vs. "statutes and ordinances"
- **EMET:** "all my statutes and all my judgments [mishpatai]"
- **LOGOS:** "all my statutes and all my rules"
- **PNEUMA:** "all my statutes and all my ordinances"
- **Ruling:** "all my statutes and all my rules" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** *Mishpatim* (plural of *mishpat*, "judgment/ruling") = case-based laws. "Rules" (LOGOS/ESV) is the most common modern formal rendering in Levitical context. "Judgments" (EMET/KJV) is archaic but accurate. "Ordinances" (PNEUMA) is also standard but less precise. "Rules" is the established convention for this project's Leviticus series.

---

### D47 — V. 23 | "I detested them" vs. "I was loathed by them"
- **EMET:** "I was loathed by them [va-aquts bam]"
- **LOGOS:** "I detested them"
- **PNEUMA:** "that is why I despised them"
- **Ruling:** "I detested them" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** *Va-aquts bam* — the verb *quts be-* ("to feel disgust/loathing toward") has YHWH as subject and the nations as object. EMET's "I was loathed by them" reads as passive (the nations loathed God), which is the opposite of the Hebrew. LOGOS's "I detested them" is correct: God is the one who detests. PNEUMA's "I despised them" is acceptable but "detested" better captures the physical-disgust sense of *quts*.

---

### D48 — V. 24 | "Inherit" vs. "possess" for yarash
- **EMET:** "You shall possess their ground [tirshу et-admatam]"
- **LOGOS:** "You shall inherit their land"
- **PNEUMA:** "you will possess their land"
- **Ruling:** "You shall inherit their land" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** *Yarash* encompasses both "to possess" and "to inherit (as a rightful possession)." "Inherit" (LOGOS) better captures the gift-character of the land — given by divine promise rather than conquered solely by human effort. "Possess" (EMET/PNEUMA) is also accurate but more neutral.

---

### D49 — V. 24 | "Ground" (adamah) vs. "land" (eretz)
- **EMET:** "their ground [admatam]" (preserving adamah vs. eretz distinction)
- **LOGOS:** "their land"
- **PNEUMA:** "their land"
- **Ruling:** "their land" (LOGOS/PNEUMA)
- **Rationale:** EMET correctly distinguishes *adamah* (ground/soil) from *eretz* (land), which is linguistically precise. However, in this inheritance context *admatam* refers to the territory of the nations — "their land" is the natural English equivalent and is what the text means. The distinction is noted but does not require separate English terms at every occurrence.

---

### D50 — V. 24 | "Distinguished" vs. "separated" for hivdil
- **EMET:** "who distinguished you [hivdalti etkhem]"
- **LOGOS:** "who has separated you"
- **PNEUMA:** "who has set you apart from all the peoples"
- **Ruling:** "who has separated you from the peoples" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** *Hivdil* (Hiphil of *badal*) = "to separate/divide/distinguish." LOGOS uses "separated" consistently for *badal* in the divine election formula (vv. 24, 26), creating terminological unity. EMET's "distinguished" is also accurate. PNEUMA adds "all" before "the peoples" — this follows the LXX Vaticanus (*apo panton ton ethnon*) but is not in the MT; the consensus follows MT without "all."

---

### D51 — V. 25 | "Creeps on the ground" vs. "with which the ground teems"
- **EMET:** "anything that creeps on the ground [ha-romes al ha-adamah]"
- **LOGOS:** "anything with which the ground teems"
- **PNEUMA:** "anything that moves along the ground"
- **Ruling:** "anything that creeps on the ground" (EMET/PNEUMA adjusted)
- **Rationale:** *Ha-romes* is the Qal participle of *ramas* ("to creep/move along the ground"), the standard term for creeping creatures in Leviticus 11. "Creeps on the ground" (EMET) is the most precise equivalent. LOGOS's "with which the ground teems" introduces the *shaqats* ("swarming") concept via "teems" but the actual word here is *romes*, not *sharats*. PNEUMA's "moves along the ground" is acceptable but "creeps" better captures the *ramas* category.

---

### D52 — V. 25 | "Make your souls detestable" vs. "make yourselves detestable"
- **EMET:** "you shall not make your souls detestable [lo teshaqtsu et-nafshoteikhem]"
- **LOGOS:** "You shall not make yourselves detestable"
- **PNEUMA:** "Do not defile yourselves"
- **Ruling:** "You shall not make yourselves detestable" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** *Lo teshaqtsu et-nafshoteikhem* — Pi'el of *shaqats* ("to make detestable") with *nafshoteikhem* ("your souls/selves") as object. LOGOS's "yourselves" is a natural English equivalent of the soul-object rendered reflexively. EMET's "your souls" is literal. PNEUMA's "defile yourselves" substitutes *tame'* vocabulary for *shaqats* vocabulary — these are distinct Hebrew categories; "detestable" (*shaqats*) is stronger and more specific than "defile" (*tame*).

---

### D53 — V. 26 | "Separated you from" vs. "distinguished you from" (repeated)
- **EMET:** "I have distinguished you from the peoples to be mine [lihyot li]"
- **LOGOS:** "have separated you from the peoples, that you should be mine"
- **PNEUMA:** "I have set you apart from the peoples so that you would be mine"
- **Ruling:** "have separated you from the peoples, that you should be mine" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** Same *badal* ruling as v. 24: "separated" is the consensus term for *badal* in the divine election formula. LOGOS maintains terminological consistency across vv. 24 and 26. PNEUMA's "set you apart" is an acceptable paraphrase but breaks the verbal unity of *badal* across the three occurrences.

---

### D54 — V. 27 | "In whom there is a medium or a spirit of the dead" vs. "who is a medium"
- **EMET:** "a man or a woman in whom there is a medium [ov] or a spirit of the dead [yid'oni]"
- **LOGOS:** "A man or a woman who is a medium or a necromancer"
- **PNEUMA:** "Any man or woman who is a medium or a spiritist"
- **Ruling:** "A man or a woman in whom there is a medium or a spirit of the dead" (EMET)
- **Rationale:** MT *asher yihyeh vahem ov o yid'oni* — "in whom there is an *ov* or a *yid'oni*." The phrase "in whom there is" is the specific MT formulation, which may imply spirit-possession or professional identity. LOGOS and PNEUMA both collapse this to "who is a medium/necromancer," converting the possession/habitation idiom to a simple identity statement. The MT's distinctive phrasing should be preserved.

---

### D55 — V. 27 | "Necromancer" vs. "spiritist" vs. "spirit of the dead" — yid'oni
- **EMET:** "spirit of the dead [yid'oni]"
- **LOGOS:** "necromancer"
- **PNEUMA:** "spiritist"
- **Ruling:** "spirit of the dead" (EMET)
- **Rationale:** This repeats the D15 ruling. *Yid'oni* ("knower") designates a familiar spirit or spirit-practitioner. "Spirit of the dead" (EMET) is the most literal. "Necromancer" (LOGOS) and "spiritist" (PNEUMA) are both interpretive. The paired rendering "mediums and spirits of the dead" maintained consistently across vv. 6 and 27.

---

### D56 — V. 27 | "Stoned with stones" vs. "they shall stone them"
- **EMET:** "with stones they shall stone them [ba-aven yirgemu otam]"
- **LOGOS:** "They shall be stoned with stones"
- **PNEUMA:** "They are to be stoned"
- **Ruling:** "they shall be stoned with stones" (LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** MT *ba-aven yirgemu otam* — the active construction "they shall stone them with stones." LOGOS renders passively ("shall be stoned with stones"), preserving *ba-aven* ("with stones"). PNEUMA drops "with stones," losing the specification. Following the parallel formula in v. 2, "with stones" is preserved. Passive construction is conventional in English for execution sentences.

---

### D57 — V. 27 | "Any man or woman" vs. "a man or a woman"
- **EMET:** "a man or a woman"
- **LOGOS:** "A man or a woman"
- **PNEUMA:** "Any man or woman"
- **Ruling:** "A man or a woman" (EMET/LOGOS)
- **Rationale:** MT *ish o ishah* = "a man or a woman." PNEUMA's "any" is an addition. The straightforward "a man or a woman" follows the MT precisely.

---

### D58 — Chapter-wide | Conditional structure: "If a man" vs. "a man who" (vv. 10–21)
- **EMET:** uses relative-clause constructions throughout ("a man who lies with," "a man who takes")
- **LOGOS:** uses conditional "If a man" throughout
- **PNEUMA:** uses conditional "If a man" throughout
- **Ruling:** "If a man..." conditional (LOGOS/PNEUMA)
- **Rationale:** The penal-code laws of vv. 10–21 use a mixture of *ish asher* (relative, "a man who") and *im* (conditional, "if") constructions in the MT. LOGOS and PNEUMA standardize to the conditional "If a man..." which is the natural English legal-code idiom for a series of penalty provisions. EMET's relative-clause form ("a man who...") is more literal to the participial Hebrew style but less natural in English legal discourse. The consensus adopts "If a man..." for the entire penal section (vv. 10–21).

## Key Theological/Lexical Disputes

1. **chesed (v. 17)** — unique negative use; "disgrace" (EMET, ruling) vs. "shameful thing" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). Most linguistically significant dispute in chapter.
2. **yid'onim (vv. 6, 27)** — "spirits of the dead" (EMET, ruling) vs. "necromancers" (LOGOS) vs. "spiritists" (PNEUMA).
3. **shakav pattern (vv. 11–20)** — "lies with" (EMET/LOGOS, ruling) vs. "sleeps with" (PNEUMA) throughout the penal code.
4. **gilui ervah formulas** — preserved in consensus against PNEUMA's consistent substitution of "violated" or "had sexual relations with."
5. **badal root (vv. 24–26)** — "separated" (LOGOS, ruling) vs. "distinguished" (EMET) vs. "set you apart" (PNEUMA).
6. **va-aquts bam (v. 23)** — "I detested them" (LOGOS, ruling) vs. EMET's misread passive "I was loathed by them."
7. **asher yihyeh vahem (v. 27)** — "in whom there is" (EMET, ruling) vs. "who is" (LOGOS/PNEUMA), preserving the possession/habitation idiom.
